title: 2025 09 03 - HE Subcommittee B (Part 2)
displayTitle: 2025-09-03 - Health Committee - Healthy Futures (Pae Ora) Amendment Bill [Subcommittee B, Part 2]
description: 3 September 2025 | Health Committee Healthy Futures (Pae Ora) Amendment Bill Rare Disorders New Zealand Te Runanga o Ngati Ruanui Trust Te Pūhana Ora Physiotherapy New Zealand Kirsten Davie, President Simon Hoar, Chief Executive Officer
descriptionSummary: Healthy Futures (Pae Ora) Amendment Bill. Rare Disorders New Zealand. Te Runanga o Ngati Ruanui Trust

chunks.text: Campbell, I'm chairing the subcommittee of the Health Select Committee. Um, we do have your, your written submission, um, and we have allocated 10 minutes for your oral submission. Um, you can use, um, that 10 minutes a a as you like. We have various members of the Health Select Committee online. We also have officials online, uh, documenting as well.

So I'll hand that time over to you now, UA Rachel ua. Um, next to me is, um, Graham Young, who will be speaking on our behalf today for Kiara. Oh Kiara. Everybody, um, Morena, um, Nati Uri welcomes the opportunity to make a submission on the Healthy Futures Paya Amendment Bill.

We acknowledge the intention of the bill to improve effectiveness of health services and the delivery of services to patients while not opposed to the intent of the Bill. Nati nu strongly opposes changes, which will undermine an equity driven approach to ensure Maori can participate and receive treatment and services within a health system that reflects our needs rather than one that reflects poor health indicators for Maori.

We say this from a point of direct involvement with the health system. Nati Rooney operates one of the largest primary healthcare practices in taranaki, providing GP care, outreach, clinical services, and whanau Aura services, not only to our own population, but to the wider community. With 9,400 registered patients, we see firsthand the impact of health equity concerns of our people struggling to interconnect with secondary care, which in turn impacts on their health outcomes.

Equally, we see positive action in some areas like the retention of comprehensive care funding, which is designed to integrate whanau back into primary healthcare and to prevent readmission to hospital care. I think we all acknowledge that more needs to be done. Perhaps the Y 2 5 7 5 tribunal report makes sober reading in respect of Maori health and some of the key issues that they highlighted was systematic crown breaches.

The tribunal found that the Crown has systematically breached its obligations under rigi in the health sector failure to protect Maori interests. The report found the Crown had failed to actively protect Maori interests and interests, Maori rights, an interest particularly regarding Maori led representation and decision making in health distortions in the health system.

The report identifies significant distortions in the New Zealand health system impacting Maori health outcomes, and more importantly, the importance of Maori led health authority. The tribunals suggested that a Maori led health authorities crucial for addressing the systematic issues and improving health outcomes for Maori.

But we know we've already lost this. Our significant concern is that this bill looks like another attempt to remove a clearly defined partnership approach, recognizing Maori in terms of our rights and interests. The changes set out in the bill are concerning in that equity models and partnership approaches are being replaced with one size fits all approach.

This one size fits all model has failed to deliver in the past. It makes no sense to continue to reinforce this through this bill. We need enabling legislation that creates equity driven solutions that deal with practical barriers, which deal with the issues of practical barriers, poor communication, a hostile healthcare environment in some cases, and primary healthcare barriers, and in particular, racism that often exist and that we confront.

It is considered that many Maori and up to 44% have unmet health needs, um, particularly in the primary health sector where we operate, which is a woeful statistic. The bill undermines all the good work in addressing the many failures identified through the Y 2 5 7 5 claim and makes, makes final changes to extinguish a fresh approach, which was envisaged in 2022 when the new health system was enacted.

All our legislative backstops are now being dismantled and diluted for what appears to be little or no specific reason. The bill does nothing to achieve effective health services delivery to Maori. The removal of the health sector principles goes to the heart of an equity driven model, and we oppose the removal, and in particular, the principle that states the health sector should be equitable, which includes ensuring Maori and other population groups have access to services in positions for their health needs, and to receive equitable levels of service

and achieve equitable health outcomes. We are concerned that removal of the above principle will impact upon how tofu order delivers services for Maori. There is no logic in respect of efficiency and effectiveness to remove such important principles.

Given the overwhelming evidence that equity driven approach that was set out in the 2 7 5 7 claim, the removal of these principles will by default maintain poor Maori statistics as proven by the historical one size fits all model of outcome. What is worrying is that this approach will eventually negatively impact on the whole of society and con continue to take the health system further into crisis.

Nati strongly opposes the changes to iwi Maori partnership boards. These changes removes the ability for Maori partnership boards to evaluate local health needs and work directly with taura. There will be a significant loss of regional voice when setting COPA and Maori investment opportunities and determining which services need to be commissioned to meet equitable outcomes for Maori.

Further, there will be an active loss of connected collaboration in respect of information and basic delivery of needs. A new layered approaches being proposed through these changes. And we don't believe this is efficient or effective at all. These changes take away the empowering local appointed iwi Maori partnership boards to engage directly at regional and community levels where real meaningful change can occur and is occurring.

Understanding what needs to occur to regional level will ensure far greater efficiencies in the overall model of care provided. Communities when communicated, uh, di uh, that communication directly occurs with ura, not via another committee and then another committee. We consider the health strategies need strong and empowered Maori health boards, uh, Maori partnership boards.

Once again, there is no real analysis that we can see as to what significant what significant efficiency or streamlining will occur when the changes proposed, uh, apart from continued disenfranchising of Maori, we consider that no case has been made to enact the changes to the role and functions of Maori partnership boards that will really help deliver meaningful changes.

In fact, we believe the opposite will occur. Nati Rooney considers this bill significantly undermines and weakens the ability to achieve prevention and population health strategies. The strong focus on equity, which is being dismantled, undermines our rights and interests, and in particular, the ti rights and interests that are so important and so foundational for us.

There is no compelling evidence that the changes proposed will create efficiencies and effectiveness and systems and structures. We seriously believe local voices will be lost amongst the new cascade of layers of reporting. We strongly recommend that Iwi Maori partnership boards are retained and that their status and direct interface with TA Aura is reinforced.

We also strongly advocate for equity driven principles to be preserved, which can only assist, uh, creating a healthier, more empowered population for everyone. Only this position will ultimately deliver the efficiencies and effectiveness of a well-functioning health system. Thank you committee.

Excellent. Thank you. Thank you very much. Graham and Rachel, do we have any questions from the committee now? We don't see any hands jumping up, Barb, but once again, please don't take that as any, any sign. Um, you have got a very, uh, concise and precise written submission, um, and we have had a chance to look through that and really appreciate you highlighting issues and your oral submission relating to that as well.

So thank you very much for, for taking the time to submit, uh, to the Health Select Committee, um, and your feedback has has been noted. So thank thank you very much. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Excellent. We'll now move to our next submitter, uh, which is T Purna Aura.
Um, do we have them online? Excellent. We have Ward Welcome Ward.

Uh, ward, if you could just unmute yourself and I've just done that. Has it got me now? Sorry. Yep, that's, that's better. We just can't see you yet. Okay. Let me just see if I can, oh, there We go. Excellent. Good to, good to see you again, ward. How are you? It's been Well. Welcome to this subcommittee of the Health Study Committee. Um, as you know, I'm Hamish Campbell.

We have various members online. Uh, we also have, uh, officials, um, online as well. Um, we have your written, written submission, um, and we have allocated 10 minutes for your oral presentation. Um, so I'll hand that time over to you. Now. Look, uh, thank you Hamish, really appreciate that.

There's really nothing in the submission, um, that shouldn't be self-evident in reading. I've tried to structure in such a way that the front end is for you, the committee to look at and the rest of it's for the, uh, officials to wade through if they so wish to do so. We're in an interesting position as an IMPB at Han. We obviously have some aspirations and things that we'd like to achieve, uh, in the Maori health space, but equally understand that, you know, that runs on the board is always what helps, uh, with regards, um, getting traction,

particularly at the funding level. One of the things that we've emphasized in here is that we're not here to argue over whether we commission it now or not. Um, if the government has made its mind up to go a certain route, we can certainly, uh, what we wish to express that we are very keen to head to the commissioning side, uh, of health here.

But in the meantime, uh, we've got a very internal focus. We want to get our information gathered 'cause we're one of the last, uh, IBS to, to, uh, come into being, uh, and to make sure that we've got the right sort of data and identification of, uh, services that will make a substantial difference we hope to the population of wa.

There are three major areas inside of the bill that I just wanna draw the committee's attention to. I think that no matter what, there should be an obligation to speak to IMP Bs. It becomes a pointless exercise to have us if there's not at least that underlying obligation, um, to talk. Um, and, uh, um, I I would ask why, why that was, um, not, uh, drafted more strongly.

Equally, um, decision making goes into HA, which I, I have to admit, I'm a slightly bit perplexed by that. It's not that I have any problem with the people on, on H Mac, but you're asking them to consolidate 17 IBS and to advice that goes through to the Ministry of Health. And I think that's a rather large ask for a group of people that are largely removed from the communities that are making, uh, recommendations.

Again, I don't want it to be seen as a criticism of the individuals on it, but rather in the process that's, that's being outlined here. And I just think that, um, over time, and it lends into our, um, desire to eventually move down that commissioning pathway. Um, and the other one is around the realities.

Um, there is a talk of cultural safety and responsiveness. I, I know those words can be triggered words at times, but if we look underneath them and actually understand what's what it's really about, it's in the light in the case of say, uh, Martin Mu and of Chatham Islands, you know, we really are an extraordinarily remu remote, uh, community.

Um, what we talk about, uh, both in cultural safety and responsiveness is the fact that we have a very particular way of being, uh, and a way of living over there. That that's, um, very much unlike anything else in the country. So when we talk, use that language to understand the remoteness, uh, of the Chatham Islands, it starts to get a bit more clarity about what's really meant, uh, with it.

We don't have immediate access to, uh, particularly to secondary care, let alone tertiary. And so, uh, a lot of what we do culturally is designed to, uh, address those matters while we wait to get people off to the hospitals, but more also in the areas of mental health, uh, and, um, areas around diabetes and lifestyle, uh, issues that we, we certainly do face, uh, on the island.

Uh, I've already said that, you know, we do want to be in a position to bid for commissioning status, particularly given our remoteness. And so I don't see any problem with adding a com a part into the act that at least enables a minister to make a decision. Uh, if an IMPB has shown it has the ability capability, uh, and the information and data required to potentially get it to that point when, when that might be, well, that's for the officials to decide how that might work if a minister was to indeed add this back into the pay order, uh, act.

And I think there just needs to be specific recognition of the remoteness. There are communities, not just the chathams, but certainly we'd probably rated as one of the most remote areas. And there are realities that go with that that perhaps could be missed out if it at least isn't recognized inside the act. Um, in such a way that that, and if you, again, if you couple it with the need to actually have the IMPB be consulted with upfront, it may just assist in mitigating some of those remote, the issues that come from being, uh,

a very remote community, uh, over in the chathams. Look, we've no doubt got a wealth of things that you need to be dealing with here. And so I'm gonna leave it at that Hamish, and if there's any questions, um, I'm willing to take them now. Excellent. Thank, thank you very much. And I do acknowledge just the remoteness of the Chathams, um, and I appreci appreciate your comments around that.

Do I, I don't see any hands up from, uh, the committee. Oh, uh, Jenny's got a hand up. Jenny Got Ward, lovely to see you. Um, now in terms of the remoteness aspect of, of the chathams and and healthcare, would you, um, would it be, are you asking for specificity around for your, um, area in particular?

Or are you talking about remoteness in terms of maybe the far north or the west coast? Are you looking purely for your area? Um, 'cause it's geographical isolation. If I, if, if I said no and I was looking more specifically, I probably wouldn't travel back to the Chatham Islands because they really do want that recognition, um, and the specificity around it.

But look, we're not the only one on the boat of being a remote location. Um, and even in the far north, um, you know, the access to services is still limited. It may still take hours to get, I mean, for us to get a life flight out, you're probably talking about a 24 hour turnaround to get somebody in. And we've managed to, you know, achieve that.

We did have a terrible situation where one of our whanau was bitten by a shark, and unfortunately we just couldn't do anything. And that person died on the island last year, I think it was. Um, but, but a number of us do face the, the remoteness and it changes the way in which we think about the delivery of health in those communities.

The chances of us getting a fully fledged, uh, hospital are not particularly great all. So what do we do then? What gets recognized inside the act that enables some of those commissioning aspects to occur on the island without the extraordinary expense that goes with trying to put in a fully fledged, um, secondary care place. But we do have a clinic there and just, you know, making sure that we've got enough service in place that we can effectively look after people as we look to locate them back, um, to the broader services available in a, in a Canterbury

or in a Wellington, uh, would be very useful. And we'd really like that recognized than this, but, but if I age any, it really goes to, um, that need to be more consultative and I think the act probably needs to be beefed up a little bit. And I've made specific recommendations on the submission. Thank you. Thank, thank you very much and thank you for your very thoughtful, thoughtful submission.

Really do, do appreciate it. Uh, we now, we now will move on to our next submitter, which is Physiotherapy New Zealand. Um, and I do believe we have Simon and Kirsten with us. Yeah. Um, first of all, thanks for the opportunity of, uh, of speaking today to the committee.

Um, yes, I'm Simon Hall. I'm the CEO of Physiotherapy New Zealand. Uh, and Simon here is Kirsten Davy, who's our board chair and president. And, uh, we represent over 4,000 physiotherapists across the tu. Um, so, so overall PNZ supports improving the effectiveness of health service delivery, um, more timely access and also enabling public private partnerships.

And so our key message today is, um, the consideration of fully utilizing the existing physio workforce and national models of care so that the bill can deliver on its intent around capacity, around timeliness and around value. Now we are really keen to push the move beyond medical centric models, uh, which predominantly rely on GPS and nurses, um, because there's a wide allied health workforce that are ready and waiting in particular physiotherapy, uh, and physiotherapists who work across a variety of clinical specialties.

So for example, physios are working in primary as well as secondary and tertiary care. So we think that to make primary care function better, first contact physio, uh, is an established model that works. And we know that musculoskeletal presentations account for up to 20% of a typical GPS workload and that redirecting them to physiotherapists in the community settings can improve access, can reduce cost, and freeze up GP time for more complex medical care.

And, and this is really, really important, particularly for our more vulnerable communities, including Maori and rural, uh, and those with disabilities as well. Uh, so alongside primary care in the secondary care space, health New Zealand already has an initiative underway to reduce orthopedic wait times in secondary care, um, by using physiotherapists for first specialist assessments.

Uh, and so fully funded end to end pathways, including community triage, first specialist assessment and rehabilitation would help to substantially reduce orthopedic wait lists and unblock the system. And we know that something like 70% of people, um, on wait lists may not require immediate specialist involvement and that physios could be part of that solution.

But it's not just musculoskeletal presentations where physios can provide solutions. Uh, we have clinical specialties across, uh, quite a broad range of areas including cardiorespiratory physios who work both pre and postoperatively. So Prehabilitation reduces post-op complications, it reduces length of stay, uh, and also readmissions.

So it's a really cost effective, um, option, which is we think currently underutilized. Um, cancer rehabilitation, another really interesting area. Um, so embedding exercise based rehabilitation improves function fatigue and return to work, which are absolutely critical for survivorship. Uh, I would also call out women's health.

So pre specialist assessment and rehabilitation is a ready to roll template similar to those musculoskeletal pathways that I talked about earlier, earlier that Health New Zealand already has underway. And finally across community teams. So comprehensive community based physiotherapy we know lowers readmissions and supports timely discharge.

So really, really critical component there. I'll just hand across to Kirsten now. Physiotherapists are ready now with solutions. We are a well qualified, we regulated, respected and an available workforce across public and private settings. There are areas where physiotherapy, public private partnerships are working well already, such as this.

The musculoskeletal initiatives, these should be expanded nationally across a variety of health conditions to ensure improved access to health services with reduced financial drag for the sector. Physiotherapists work seamlessly with primary care teams and we refer into the secondary and tertiary care areas only as needed.

This is a more cost effective pathway, enabling better access to effective treatment for people within their communities. Physiotherapists can certainly help achieve the health targets where experts in rehabilitation enabling timely return to function and work, which reduces downstream costs.

If we look at emergency departments, we know that acute musculoskeletal conditions account for around 20% of people sitting and waiting in the ED departments. Physiotherapists can support the shortest stays in ED target through working in those departments and ensuring people presenting with those musculoskeletal and painful conditions are assessed and discharged in a timely manner.

If we look at inpatient care, we know that adequately resourcing of inpatient physios can have a significant reduction in hospital length of stay. And examples where people with hi hip fractures, if they're mobilized on day one, uh, it results in them leaving hospital on average two days earlier.

So it reduces the cost of their hospitals stay. Um, and so that is obviously only able to be achieved if there a physiotherapist available to do that. The bottom line is that physiotherapists are available. These sorts of initiatives are immediately scalable and will help, um, access, they'll help reduce wait times, they'll help the hospital flow with that.

Right. Commissioning and funding from an equity and I'm sorry. Yeah, and so, so there's a few things for us in terms of what needs to be in the bill. Um, so firstly, we, we really humbly request, uh, explicitly including Allied health and in particular physiotherapy and national models of care and commissioning guidance, not just medical nursing, um, to also enable and expect Health New Zealand to partner with both private and community providers, including in the rehabilitation space and to also set targets that include rehabilitation.

So whether that's cancer care, cardio, respiratory, other areas, um, it will be really powerful. Um, we, we also just wanted to highlight, we think there's a risk with repealing the health sector principles and the New Zealand Health Charter in terms of unintended consequences, uh, if we weaken the culture of accountability and put, uh, outcomes at risk.

Um, so really that distribution of expertise and and retention is a really challenge, uh, that our members are, are already reporting. Um, and so we recommend the bill retains or replaces these with enforceable expectations for supportive working culture, for manageable workloads and rostering for safe physical demands and ongoing professional development, which are all determinants of greater, better patient outcomes.

And just finally, physiotherapy, New Zealand does support equitable service design with strengthened iwi Maori roles. We are concerned that timely access for Maori could worsen if Allied Health is not explicitly included. Physiotherapy must be embedded in primary and community models, and we look forward to working alongside our Maori providers.

So we ask the committee to direct Health New Zealand to fully utilize physiotherapists as first contact practitioners and primary care for musculoskeletal conditions and long-term conditions, and expand that first specialist assessment pathways and that type of initiative, embedding rehabilitation targets into the models of care such as cancer and cardio, respiratory, women's health ed.

The list can go on and on as to how we can assist, uh, health New Zealand Commission community physiotherapy via public private interface partnerships across the mall too, uh, with funding to match the demand and clear referral pathways, um, from GPS and into ed resource inpatient physiotherapy.

So we can appropriately assist in reaching the health targets and future health targets, obviously, as well include rehabilitation spaces and infrastructure programs within Health New Zealand. And as Simon has just, uh, alluded to retaining and strengthening the health sector principles in the New Zealand charter to ensure, um, health workforce wellbeing and accountability and as I've just mentioned as well, strengthening equity partnering with iwi Maori providers and ensure timely access to physiotherapy in Maori communities.

Kira, we are ready to help deliver capacity and timeliness. Now, physiotherapists are there. We are shovel ready. We are willing, um, to work with you and, um, for the best, uh, for the New Zealand public. We welcome your questions. Excellent. Thank, thank you very much to the both of you.

Um, I don't see any hands up from, from the committee at this stage, so I just, that leads me just to thank you for, uh, your written and oral submissions. We really do appreciate, appreciate your feedback, um, and the time you've taken to submit to the committee. So thank, thank you very much. Um, thank you.

We now are scheduled to have a break, um, to one o'clock. Um, so if, if members can be back uh, by one o'clock, that'd be most appreciated. So, uh, it's been a fairly full on morning. Um, I appreciate, I might have had the hours wrong when I announced the last break. Um, that's because it's probably been been a busy morning.
I.
